steps.dialogue: You've learned that equivalent fractions share the same position. This idea will help us next time when we compare different fractions. Let's try applying what you've discovered!
Which fraction is equivalent to [event:697feee893dc027b] [fraction numerator=1 denominator=4]one fourth[/fraction]? Can you place it [event:697feefa054a0632] on the number line?

prompt.text: Place the fraction that equals [fraction numerator=1 denominator=4]one fourth[/fraction] onto the number line.

step.dialogue: [event:restore_snapshot_prompt_start]Let's try again. Count where [fraction numerator=1 denominator=4]one fourth[/fraction] goes, what fraction does it match on the bottom line?

metadata.description: Eighths marks appear on the number line

step.dialogue: [event:restore_snapshot_prompt_start]Not quite. Here's a hint: [fraction numerator=1 denominator=4]one fourth[/fraction] is [event:697fecf0bbaab2c7] here. Let's count eighths: [event:6980f80b97768341] [fraction numerator=1 denominator=8]one eighth[/fraction], [fraction numerator=2 denominator=8]two eighths[/fraction] lands at the [event:6980f93378198375] same spot.

metadata.description: Animated counting showing 2/8 landing at same position as 1/4

step.dialogue: [event:restore_snapshot_prompt_start]Let's work through this together. [fraction numerator=1 denominator=4]One fourth[/fraction] is at  [event:697fecf0bbaab2c7] this point. Now lets count eighths: [event:6980fa20cec58a18] [fraction numerator=1 denominator=8]one eighth[/fraction] is halfway to [fraction numerator=1 denominator=4]one fourth[/fraction], [event:6980fa4a47919eb0] [fraction numerator=2 denominator=8]two eighths[/fraction] reaches exactly where [event:6980fa9f4f87b8df] [fraction numerator=1 denominator=4]one fourth[/fraction] is. So [event:6980fad26043df98] [fraction numerator=2 denominator=8]two eighths[/fraction] equals [fraction numerator=1 denominator=4]one fourth[/fraction].

on_correct.dialogue: Right! You figured out that [event:6980090c7973a2d4]  [fraction numerator=2 denominator=8]two eighths[/fraction] equals [event:69800960431aaaaa] [fraction numerator=1 denominator=4]one fourth[/fraction]!

steps.dialogue: This bar shows [event:6980daa59e35d008] [fraction numerator=3 denominator=3]three thirds[/fraction]. Where does [fraction numerator=3 denominator=3]three thirds[/fraction] land on the number line?

prompt.text: Place a point at [fraction numerator=3 denominator=3]three thirds[/fraction] on the number line.

step.dialogue: Almost. [fraction numerator=3 denominator=3]Three thirds[/fraction] means all 3 parts out of 3, that equals 1.

metadata.description: All 3 parts flash in the bar

step.dialogue: [event:restore_snapshot_prompt_start] Here's how I think through this: You have [event:6980e12edbdead3a] 3 parts and you're taking all 3, that's [event:6980e39c57d3d646] everything, which equals 1.

metadata.description: Counting animation showing each third, landing at 1

step.dialogue: [event:restore_snapshot_prompt_start] Let's work through this together. Count: [event:6980dba7c3778738] [fraction numerator=1 denominator=3]one third[/fraction], [fraction numerator=2 denominator=3]two thirds[/fraction], [fraction numerator=3 denominator=3]three thirds[/fraction]. We have [event:6980de3366210d8b] all three parts out of three, that's the complete whole, which equals 1.

on_correct.dialogue: That's right! You know that [event:6980e4dd424f6ff0] [fraction numerator=3 denominator=3]three thirds[/fraction] equals [event:6980e787ee057cc6]1.

steps.dialogue: Look at the top line, it shows [event:6980e9faeb305561] [fraction numerator=4 denominator=3]four thirds[/fraction]. Which fraction from the bank is equivalent to [event:6980ea1669c867e9] [fraction numerator=4 denominator=3]four thirds[/fraction]? place it on the bottom line at the same position.

prompt.text: Place the fraction that equals [fraction numerator=4 denominator=3]four thirds[/fraction] onto the number line.

step.dialogue: Let's think about this. [fraction numerator=4 denominator=3]Four thirds[/fraction] lands here on the top line. Which fraction is equivalent? Remember, equivalent fractions share the same position.

[event:restore_snapshot_prompt_start] You're working on it. Here's a method that helps: [fraction numerator=4 denominator=3]four thirds[/fraction] is four [d:0.1] thirds. Lets count: [event:6980ea556e6cc051] [fraction numerator=3 denominator=3]three thirds[/fraction] equals 1, [fraction numerator=4 denominator=3]four thirds[/fraction] is [event:6980ead8d363c597] one more fourth past that. Now [event:6980eb0e6d976ec6] think: for [fraction numerator=8 denominator=6]eight sixths[/fraction],[d:0.1] [event:6980eb9672fd1296] [fraction numerator=6 denominator=6]six sixths[/fraction] equals 1, then [fraction numerator=7 denominator=6]seven sixths[/fraction], [fraction numerator=8 denominator=6]eight sixths[/fraction]. Thats the same position!

metadata.description: Animated counting on both lines showing 8/6 aligning with 4/3

step.dialogue: [event:restore_snapshot_prompt_start] Let me show you. [fraction numerator=4 denominator=3]Four thirds[/fraction] on the top lands [event:6980f06ea0f83361] here, past [event:6980f251154fef9f] 1. For [fraction numerator=8 denominator=6]eight sixths[/fraction] on the bottom. count like this:  [event:6980efd42f5620e7] [fraction numerator=6 denominator=6]six sixths[/fraction] equals 1, [fraction numerator=7 denominator=6]seven sixths[/fraction], [fraction numerator=8 denominator=6]eight sixths[/fraction], that lands at the same spot as [fraction numerator=4 denominator=3]Four thirds![/fraction] [event:6980f0211a782ffd]. When you double numerator and denominator, you get an equivalent fraction at the same position.

on_correct.dialogue: Excellent! You recognized that [fraction numerator=8 denominator=6]eight sixths[/fraction] is [event:6980f34b2fc97b24] equivalent to [fraction numerator=4 denominator=3]four thirds[/fraction]. They both land at the [event:6980f3af64a73440] same position, that proves they're equivalent!

steps.dialogue: Great work! You're showing that you really understand how position shows equivalence!
